key_insight: Stearic-acid modification makes alumina nanoparticles much more hydrophobic, and when combined with PDMS in a spray-coated multilayer film, it can produce superhydrophobic surfaces.
engineering_relevance: This offers a scalable, low-complexity route to fabricate water-repellent coatings for glass and other surfaces using a spray process.
method: Modified alumina nanoparticles with stearic acid dissolved in ethanol using soaking or refluxing.
Compared modification routes by thermal behavior and functional groups; refluxing gave higher grafting density.
Mixed reflux-treated particles with PDMS and spray coated multilayer films on glass.
evidence_signal: The best coating was 4 layers with 10 wt% SA-modified alumina, giving a 150.0° water contact angle and 6.2° contact angle hysteresis, plus self-cleaning behavior.
watch_item: Higher coating-layer counts improve water repellency but reduce optical transmittance because of increased light scattering, so transparency must be traded against hydrophobicity.
paper_title: Facile stearic acid modifications of alumina nanoparticles for superhydrophobic coating
